Image descriptions:
1. A spiral galaxy shown in mid-infrared light. The image is dominated by an extremely bright glow from the galaxy’s nucleus. Six large and two smaller rays of light emit from the center, which are diffraction spikes created by the telescope’s optics. The galaxy’s spiral arms are visible by two lines of glowing orange bubbles which whirl out into the disc. Swirling blue clouds of dust make up the rest of the galaxy.
 
2. A spiral galaxy shown in near-infrared light. Six long, thin rays of light emit from the center, which are diffraction spikes created by the telescope’s optics. A glowing bar spans across the center. A glittering orange ring of stars and dust surrounds the bar; at each side, the ring splits off into a spiral arm that winds outwards, traced by dark red dust and more glowing orange spots. The galaxy’s disc is a pale glow.
 
3. A spiral galaxy shown in infrared light. Six long and two smaller rays of light emit from the center, which are diffraction spikes created by the telescope’s optics. A glowing bar spans across the center. A glittering ring of stars and dust surrounds the bar; at each side, the ring splits off into a spiral arm that winds outwards. Faint, dark red dust clouds swirl throughout the rest of the disc, backed by a pale glow from all the galaxy’s stars.
Such a masterpiece to start my day with💫

text:
I love migrations. This is incredible work. Hard to even comprehend a migration this massive still exists today. The aerial perspective really shows the scale and movement in such a powerful way.

Also such an important reminder that conservation is rarely black and white. Peace, human survival, development, and wildlife protection are all colliding at the same time here. Really thoughtful storytelling.
South Sudan 🇸🇸🇸🇸🇸🇸 the youngest nation in the world proud to be ❤️❤️❤️🙌🙌 East Africa 🌍

This migration and broader landscape are an extraordinary reminder of what still exists in our natural world, and why collaboration, conservation, and the long-term conserving of these ecosystems matter so deeply. Thank you for sharing this powerful story.
